Glossary About
Antiquity

Before Plotinus

1st c. BCE – 2nd c. CE

Middle Platonism, Philo, and the Peripatetic retrieval that sets the stage for Neoplatonism.

Yes. Your current set already gives four major poles in the pre-Plotinian debate: Platonic immortality and psychic rule, Aristotelian hylomorphism, Stoic corporeal pneuma, and Alexander’s sharpened Aristotelianism. What it still lacks are the main alternatives and mediations around those poles: the medical tradition, the Epicurean line, and the Middle-Platonic bridge to Plotinus, plus several fragmentary predecessors who generate the options later authors inherit or reject.

The indispensable additions are, in my judgment, the Hippocratic On the Sacred Disease; Epicurus and Lucretius; Galen; Plutarch; Alcinous; and, as a fragmentary but very important bridge to Plotinus, Numenius. Behind them stand the earlier fragmentary figures Alcmaeon, Empedocles, Anaxagoras, Heraclitus, Diogenes of Apollonia, Democritus, and the fragment attributed to Philolaus. For some of those early authors, “primary source” means fragments plus the ancient testimonia that preserve them.

The additions that most change the map

The Hippocratic corpus, above all On the Sacred Disease.
This is one of the most important missing bodies of evidence because it gives you a naturalized, medical account of mind before and alongside the philosophers. In On the Sacred Disease, the brain is the central organ of awareness and intelligence: through it we think, understand, see, hear, and distinguish good from bad, pleasant from unpleasant, just from unjust. The same treatise also locates pleasures, laughter, grief, pains, madness, fears, and delirium in bodily conditions centered on the brain. Its great polemical move is to deny that epilepsy is “more divine” than other illnesses. What looks spiritually exceptional is still bodily disease. That is a major ancient option your present dossier does not yet represent: not soul as immortal knower, nor soul as form, nor soul as pneuma, but mind as medically intelligible pathology.

The Hippocratic Regimen tradition also matters, especially the dream material. There dreams are not treated primarily as disclosures of a disembodied soul, but as diagnostic signs of bodily condition. Regimen, environment, and bodily balance shape psychic experience. That line runs forward into Galen and stands behind a great deal of later ancient psychophysiology.

Epicurus and Lucretius.
This is the biggest missing Hellenistic alternative. Without Epicureanism, the Hellenistic map is badly lopsided, because you have Stoic corporealism but not atomist corporealism. In Epicurus’ Letter to Herodotus (especially 63–68), soul is a body: a compound of exceedingly fine particles distributed through the organism, comparable to breath and heat but containing a still finer unnamed component. Soul is the principal cause of sensation, but it does not sense apart from the living organism; nor can the body sense without soul. When the bodily compound dissolves, the soul disperses and sensation ceases. That is the ontological basis of the ethical conclusion in Letter to Menoeceus and Principal Doctrine 2: death is nothing to us, because all good and bad consist in sensation, and death is privation of sensation.

Lucretius’ De rerum natura Books 3 and 4 are then indispensable because they are the fullest surviving ancient materialist psychology. Book 3 distinguishes animus and anima: the mind as ruling center, located in the chest, and the soul or vital spirit diffused through the limbs and whole body. But the distinction does not create two substances; they form one bodily nature. Lucretius argues at length that both are corporeal, both are born with the body, both grow and weaken with it, both are altered by wine, disease, fear, and age, and both perish. He uses precisely the kinds of phenomena other schools also cared about—injury, drunkenness, sleep, illness, emotional shock—to show mortality rather than immortality. Book 4 adds the Epicurean theory of mental imaging: thought and dreams are caused by extraordinarily fine films or simulacra striking the mind. So even imagination is materialized.

What Epicurus and Lucretius give you, in primary sources, is the cleanest ancient denial that mind or soul survives bodily death, and one of the clearest attempts to explain sensation, emotion, and cognition without any separable psychic principle.

Galen.
Galen is probably the single most important missing author once you move past the classical schools. He belongs in the dossier not as a mere physician but as a full-scale theorist of soul and mind working directly against Stoics, while appropriating Plato, Hippocrates, and parts of Aristotle. His On the Doctrines of Hippocrates and Plato is the key text. There he argues for a genuinely tripartite psychology: rational faculty in the brain, spirited faculty in the heart, appetitive faculty in the liver. That is Timaean-Platonic organ localization turned into medical-philosophical doctrine. It is also an explicit anti-Stoic move. Against Chrysippus, Galen refuses to collapse passions into judgments in a single ruling part. Anger and appetite are not just bad reasoning; they belong to distinct psychic powers, and bodily evidence tracks their operations.

Galen’s anatomical writings then reinforce the point. In works like On the Usefulness of the Parts and the anatomical treatises, he argues from nerves, spinal cord, and brain lesions that sensation and voluntary motion depend centrally on the brain and its extensions. His strongest empirical case is for brain-centered sensation and motor control; the heart and liver localizations are more Platonizing than experimental. Still, taken together, his corpus gives the most sophisticated ancient attempt to combine anatomy, clinical observation, and philosophical psychology.

Then comes That the Capacities of the Soul Follow the Mixtures of the Body. This is a major text for any ancient history of mind. Galen argues that psychic capacities follow bodily temperaments or mixtures: age, diet, climate, sleep, disease, wine, humoral states, and especially pathological mixtures alter character and cognition. The point is not trivial influence but deep dependence. Intellectual sharpness, emotional volatility, sluggishness, courage, melancholy, and other traits track the body’s constitution. Yet Galen does not become a simple reductive materialist. In On My Own Opinions, he explicitly withholds judgment about the ultimate substance of the rational soul and about whether it is mortal or immortal. His certainty is about psychophysical dependence, not about final metaphysical settlement.

That combination makes him crucial. He is neither a pure Platonist nor a Stoic nor an Aristotelian. He is a medically armed Platonist with strong physiological commitments and an unusual degree of metaphysical caution.

Plutarch.
Plutarch is the most substantial extant Platonist between Plato and Plotinus, and he is missing if your goal is to understand what “Platonism on soul” looked like after Plato but before Neoplatonism. Three texts matter most.

In On Moral Virtue, Plutarch attacks the Stoic treatment of passion and insists that the irrational part of the soul is real. Moral virtue is not the eradication of passion, nor merely the cognitive correction of mistaken judgments. It is the measured ordering of the non-rational soul under reason. On this point he is closer to Aristotle than to the Stoics, but he remains recognizably Platonic in maintaining a real, structured irrational element.

In On the Generation of the Soul in the Timaeus, Plutarch gives a cosmological psychology. He insists that the Timaeus is speaking of a genuinely generated world-soul, not a merely pedagogical or logical construction. He reads the text as describing rational order imposed upon prior disorderly self-motion. That matters because it makes soul, even world-soul, a mixed and dynamic reality rather than a purely static intellectual principle.

In On the Face in the Moon, Plutarch gives one of the richest pre-Plotinian eschatological psychologies. Human beings are effectively triadic: body, soul, and mind. Death comes in two stages. The first separates body from soul; the second, associated with the moon, separates mind from soul. He even links the human components to the cosmic bodies: body to earth, soul to moon, mind to sun. That is a very important pre-Plotinian distinction between psyche and nous.

Alcinous.
Alcinous’ Didaskalikos is less original than Plutarch, but it is enormously useful because it shows you what school-Platonism looked like in handbook form. He presents the soul as an incorporeal, self-moving cause of motion, places it within an ensouled cosmos, preserves the Platonic hierarchy of rational, spirited, and appetitive powers, and orients ethics around the soul’s assimilation to god through virtue and contemplation. The value of Alcinous is not novelty. It is that he gives you a compact, systematic version of the sort of Platonist psychology Plotinus inherits as living doctrine, not merely as the text of Plato itself.

Numenius.
Numenius is fragmentary, but if the destination is Plotinus he is too important to leave out. The surviving fragments, preserved in authors such as Eusebius, Proclus, and Calcidius, point to a Platonism more sharply dualized and more noetic than Plutarch’s. He strongly separates the divine and intelligible from the material realm, treats embodiment as entanglement and division, and the testimonia suggest a split between higher rational life and lower soul-life bound up with matter. His theology of first and second god is not itself a doctrine of soul, but it matters because it sharpens the hierarchy above soul and helps explain why later Platonists so strongly distinguish nous from psyche. Because the evidence is fragmentary, I would use Numenius as a bridge dossier rather than as a foundational one. But he belongs in the map.

The fragmentary background that generates the later options

At the lexical background level, Homer matters because psychē is not yet the single bearer of all mental life; thymos and noos do a great deal of that work in the epics. But the first real philosophical dossiers begin with the Presocratics and the medical writers.

Alcmaeon of Croton.
Alcmaeon is one of the most important early missing names. Aristotle reports in De anima I.2 that Alcmaeon calls the soul immortal because it is always in motion, comparing it to the heavenly bodies that are forever moving. That is a clear antecedent to the Platonic linkage between soul, self-motion, and immortality. But Alcmaeon is equally important for another reason: in Theophrastus’ De sensibus, he is presented as making the brain the center of sensation and understanding. The sensory channels lead to the brain, and humans differ from animals in that humans understand while animals merely perceive. So in one figure you already have two lines that later antiquity keeps apart and recombines: ever-moving soul and encephalocentric psychology.

The fragment attributed to Philolaus.
The fragmentary evidence attributed to Philolaus is thin and must be handled cautiously, but it is relevant. In the famous bodily-distribution fragment, intelligence is associated with the brain, soul and sensation with the heart, growth with the navel, and generation with the sexual organs. Even if one treats the text cautiously, it is an important precursor to later bodily localization of psychic functions in the Timaeus and Galen.

Heraclitus.
Heraclitus is not a systematic psychologist, but his fragments matter. The soul has an extraordinary depth and logos; one would not find its limits by traveling every road. He also links moral and cognitive quality to bodily state: the “dry” soul is wisest and best, while moistening the soul is associated with drunkenness and diminished control. He further places soul within a wider process of elemental transformation. So already in Heraclitus the soul is at once deep, logos-bearing, and physiologically qualified.

Empedocles.
Empedocles is one of the decisive early sources for later discussions because he combines transmigration with strong physiology. In the Purifications fragments, the soul or daimon is exiled, punished, and passed through many embodiments; he famously says he has already been boy, girl, bush, bird, and fish. That line feeds directly into later Platonic and Pythagorean understandings of transmigration. But Empedocles is not simply “mystical.” In the physical fragments and in Aristotle’s reports, thought is located in the blood around the heart, and cognition works by “like knowing like”: earth by earth, water by water, love by love, strife by strife. He is therefore a striking combination of reincarnating soul and cardiocentric physiology.

Anaxagoras.
Anaxagoras matters more for mind than for soul in the ordinary sense, but he is indispensable once the question becomes nous. In the great fragment on Nous, mind is infinite, self-ruling, mixed with nothing else, the finest and purest of things, knowing all and having the greatest power; it is what set the cosmic rotation in motion. This is one of the earliest and strongest Greek claims that the highest mind is not just another material ingredient in the mixture. Later Aristotelian and Platonist doctrines of intellect are unimaginable without this background.

Diogenes of Apollonia.
Diogenes is a major precursor to Stoic pneuma. In his surviving fragments, air is the underlying reality, and this same air is what gives life and intelligence to animals and human beings. It is divine, governing, and present in all things, though in different conditions. The later Stoic identification of soul with intelligent breath does not come out of nowhere; Diogenes is one of the clearest antecedents.

Democritus.
Democritus gives you the atomist precursor to Epicurus. Aristotle reports in De anima that Democritus makes soul and mind out of fine, spherical, fire-like atoms, whose ceaseless motion explains animation and cognition; breathing replenishes the soul-atoms. The important point is not every detail of the lost theory, but the option it creates: soul and mind as fully material, finitely organized, and ultimately dispersible.

Smaller but still real omissions

Theophrastus.
Theophrastus matters less as a rival grand theory than as Aristotle’s immediate successor and as the great recorder of earlier perceptual theories. His De sensibus is indispensable for the history of perception and for preserving earlier doctrines about soul, sensation, and the organs. The fragments of his lost On the Soul also show that Aristotelian psychology did not stop with Aristotle. He belongs in the dossier, though after the larger additions above.

Dicaearchus and Aristoxenus.
These are important because they keep alive reductionist or harmony-based accounts of soul. The evidence is indirect, preserved especially by Cicero and other later witnesses, but it is relevant precisely because Plato’s Phaedo and later Aristotelians attack these positions. Aristoxenus associates soul with a kind of bodily attunement or harmony; Dicaearchus seems, in the testimonia, to reduce soul to bodily function or condition rather than distinct substance. These views show that “soul as harmony” was not merely a Platonic thought-experiment; it remained a live ancient option.

Cicero as witness.
Cicero is not, on this topic, as original as Galen or Plutarch. But Tusculan Disputations I is an important primary witness for Roman-era arguments about immortality, for Peripatetic reductionist positions, and for the way Platonic, Academic, and Peripatetic theories of soul were received in Latin philosophical prose. He is often the easiest surviving source for lost discussions.

Bottom line

For a genuinely balanced pre-Plotinian history of soul/mind, the main missing pieces are these. First, the medical-naturalist line: Hippocratic writers and then Galen. Second, the Epicurean line: Epicurus and Lucretius. Third, the Middle-Platonic bridge: Plutarch, Alcinous, and fragmentary Numenius. Fourth, the fragmentary predecessors who generate the later menu of possibilities: Alcmaeon, Empedocles, Anaxagoras, Heraclitus, Diogenes of Apollonia, Democritus, and probably Philolaus.

That expansion gives you, before Plotinus, nearly every major live option in the ancient debate: soul as immortal and ever-moving; soul as exiled daimon; soul as brain-centered awareness; soul as heart-centered blood; soul as air; soul as atoms; soul as hylomorphic form; soul as corporeal pneuma; soul as medically body-dependent capacity; and soul as a layered Platonic reality rising toward nous. Without those additions, the map is missing too much of the terrain Plotinus actually inherited.
